expanded_description: Master the systematic rules for naming binary and simple covalent (molecular) compounds using Greek prefixes, and learn to write correct molecular formulas from compound names.

core_concept.explanation: Unlike ionic compounds, covalent compounds are named using Greek prefixes to indicate the number of atoms of each element. The first element keeps its element name; the second element uses the root name with the suffix '-ide'. Prefixes specify atom counts, and the prefix 'mono-' is omitted for the first element only.

image.alt: Flowchart for naming covalent compounds using Greek prefixes
image.caption: Figure 6.3.1: Decision process for naming binary covalent compounds—identify elements, count atoms, apply prefixes, modify second element to '-ide'.

sections.introduction: Covalent compound names must specify how many atoms of each element are present. Greek prefixes provide this quantitative information systematically.

key_concept.definition: Greek prefixes indicate the number of atoms of each element in a covalent molecule: mono- (1), di- (2), tri- (3), tetra- (4), penta- (5), hexa- (6), hepta- (7), octa- (8), nona- (9), deca- (10).
key_concept.context: The prefix 'mono-' is never used for the first element but is required for the second element when only one atom is present.

characteristics: First element: element name, omit 'mono-' if count = 1
Second element: root + '-ide', always include prefix
Drop final vowel of prefix before vowel-starting element (e.g., 'monooxide' $\rightarrow$ 'monoxide')
classifications.behavior: Prefixes directly modify the element names to convey stoichiometry without subscripts in the name.

characteristics: Drop 'a' or 'o' from prefix before element starting with vowel
classifications.behavior: Prevents awkward double-vowel combinations while preserving meaning.

examples.explanation: Sulfur + trioxide (not trioxide with extra 'i') $\rightarrow$ Sulfur trioxide

principles.statement: The element with the lower group number (or higher period if same group) is named first, except hydrogen is usually named first when bonded to nonmetals.
principles.application: In $\text{NF}_3$, nitrogen (Group 15) comes before fluorine (Group 17) $\rightarrow$ Nitrogen trifluoride.
principles.analogy: Like alphabetical order but based on periodic table position—the 'earlier' element leads the name.

sections.introduction: A systematic approach ensures accurate naming of any binary covalent compound from its formula.

key_concept.definition: Binary covalent compounds contain exactly two different nonmetal elements bonded covalently.
key_concept.context: These naming rules apply only to covalent compounds; ionic compounds follow different conventions (no prefixes).

components.interpretation: Brackets indicate optional 'mono-' for first element only; all other prefixes are mandatory.

image.alt: Flowchart of covalent compound naming steps
image.caption: Figure 6.3.2: Algorithm for converting formula to name.

categories.description: Determine the two elements and confirm the first element is the one with lower group number (or higher period).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Use periodic table position: left/bottom elements named first; hydrogen usually first except with Group 16/17.

image.caption: Periodic table positioning guides naming order.

categories.type: Step 2: Count Atoms and Assign Prefixes
categories.description: Count atoms of each element from subscripts; assign corresponding Greek prefix.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Subscript = atom count; no subscript = 1 atom = 'mono-' (for second element only).

image.caption: Subscripts directly map to Greek prefixes.

categories.description: Replace the ending of the second element's name with '-ide'.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Standard suffix change: oxygen $\rightarrow$ oxide, chlorine $\rightarrow$ chloride, sulfur $\rightarrow$ sulfide, etc.
Remove final 'a' or 'o' from prefix before vowel-starting element name; keep 'mono-' as 'mon-' only before 'o'.

principles.statement: Every subscript in the formula must be reflected by a prefix in the name (except 'mono-' for first element).
principles.example: Missing a prefix implies incorrect stoichiometry communication.

sections.section_title: Writing Formulas from Covalent Compound Names
sections.introduction: The reverse process—converting a systematic name to a chemical formula—requires decoding prefixes into subscripts.

key_concept.definition: Greek prefixes in the name directly indicate the subscript for each element in the molecular formula.
key_concept.context: No charge balancing is needed (unlike ionic compounds); prefixes give exact atom counts.

characteristics: mono- = 1, di- = 2, tri- = 3, tetra- = 4, penta- = 5, hexa- = 6, hepta- = 7, octa- = 8, nona- = 9, deca- = 10
No prefix on first element = subscript 1
Always write subscripts as numbers, not words
classifications.behavior: Each prefix is converted to its numerical value and placed as a subscript after the element symbol.

characteristics: First-named element written first in formula
classifications.behavior: The linguistic order in the name matches the symbolic order in the formula.

principles.statement: Covalent molecular formulas represent actual molecule composition; subscripts are never reduced to lowest terms.
principles.application: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$ is dinitrogen tetroxide, NOT $\text{NO}_2$ (which is nitrogen dioxide, a different compound).
principles.analogy: Like a recipe: '2 eggs, 4 cups flour' is different from '1 egg, 2 cups flour'—you can't simplify the ratio and keep the same result.

sections.introduction: Some covalent compounds have traditional names that persist alongside systematic names, and a few require special handling.

key_concept.definition: While systematic naming is preferred, certain compounds are universally known by common names that must be memorized.
key_concept.context: Recognizing both systematic and common names is essential for chemical literacy.

categories.description: Universal common names that override systematic nomenclature.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Historical usage and ubiquity cement these names in chemical language.

examples.explanation: Systematic: Dihydrogen monoxide; Common: Water (always use 'water').
Systematic: Nitrogen trihydride; Common: Ammonia (always use 'ammonia').

categories.description: Hydrogen bonded to Group 16/17 elements often use 'hydrogen [element]' naming.
categories.detailed_mechanism: These compounds exhibit acidic behavior in water, influencing naming convention.

examples.explanation: Systematic: Hydrogen chloride; Also accepted: Hydrogen chloride (no prefix style).
Systematic: Dihydrogen monosulfide; Common: Hydrogen sulfide.

categories.description: Simple carbon-hydrogen compounds follow organic nomenclature, not binary covalent rules.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Methane, ethane, etc., use IUPAC organic naming, covered in later units.

examples.explanation: Not 'carbon tetrahydride'—systematic name is Methane (organic nomenclature).

principles.statement: Use systematic prefix naming for binary nonmetal compounds unless a universally accepted common name exists.
principles.application: Name $\text{CO}_2$ as carbon dioxide (systematic), but name $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ as water (common).
principles.analogy: Like addressing someone: use their formal title in official documents, but their nickname among friends—both are correct in context.

sections.introduction: Apply covalent nomenclature rules to convert between names and formulas with confidence.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Binary Covalent Compound

step_by_step.explanation: P (Group 15) and O (Group 16); P has lower group number, so named first.
Subscript 2 on P $\rightarrow$ 'di-'; subscript 5 on O $\rightarrow$ 'penta-'.
'Penta-' ends in 'a', 'oxide' starts with 'o' $\rightarrow$ drop 'a': pentoxide.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Binary Covalent Nomenclature with Prefix Rules

worked_examples.title: Writing a Formula from a Name
worked_examples.problem: Write the formula for sulfur trioxide.

step_by_step.explanation: S has no prefix $\rightarrow$ subscript 1; 'tri-' = 3 $\rightarrow$ O subscript 3.
Sulfur named first $\rightarrow$ S written first; oxygen second $\rightarrow$ O written second.

worked_examples.title: Handling Vowel Dropping and 'Mono-' Exceptions
worked_examples.problem: Name the compound $\text{Cl}_2\text{O}$ and explain why it is not 'dichlorine monooxide'.

step_by_step.explanation: 'Mono-' before 'oxide' (starts with 'o') drops the 'o' in 'mono' but keeps the 'o' in 'monoxide' for pronunciation.
step_by_step.calculation: mono- + oxide $\rightarrow$ monoxide (not monooxide, not monxide)

worked_examples.final_answer: Dichlorine monoxide. The prefix 'mono-' loses its final 'o' before the vowel-starting 'oxide', but the convention retains 'monoxide' (not 'monxide') for clarity and pronunciation.

worked_examples.title: Distinguishing Covalent vs. Ionic Naming
worked_examples.problem: Why is $\text{CO}$ named carbon monoxide (with prefix) but $\text{NaCl}$ named sodium chloride (no prefix)?

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{CO}$: C and O are both nonmetals $\rightarrow$ covalent; $\text{NaCl}$: Na is metal, Cl is nonmetal $\rightarrow$ ionic.
Covalent compounds use Greek prefixes to specify atom counts; ionic compounds rely on charge balance, so prefixes are unnecessary and incorrect.
step_by_step.calculation: Covalent: prefixes required; Ionic: no prefixes

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{CO}$: covalent $\rightarrow$ carbon monoxide (prefix for O); $\text{NaCl}$: ionic $\rightarrow$ sodium chloride (no prefix, charges balance implicitly).

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{CO}$ is covalent (two nonmetals), so prefixes specify the 1:1 ratio: carbon monoxide. $\text{NaCl}$ is ionic (metal + nonmetal); the 1:1 ratio is implied by charge balance ($\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$), so no prefix is used: sodium chloride. Using prefixes for ionic compounds is incorrect.

summary: Covalent compounds are named using Greek prefixes to indicate atom counts: mono- (1), di- (2), tri- (3), etc. The first element keeps its name (omit 'mono-' if count = 1); the second element uses root + '-ide' with a prefix always included. Vowel dropping simplifies pronunciation (e.g., 'pentoxide' not 'pentaoxide'). To write formulas from names, convert prefixes directly to subscripts. Unlike ionic compounds, covalent formulas are never simplified. Common names like 'water' ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) and 'ammonia' ($\text{NH}_3$) override systematic names. Correct nomenclature depends on identifying bonding type: prefixes for covalent, charge balance for ionic.
